text:
China’s export controls on indium phosphide are creating a new bottleneck for AI data center expansion worldwide. The material is essential for high-speed optical chips that use photonics to move data through fiber instead of copper, a technology companies are racing to adopt as AI workloads outpace traditional electrical interconnects. Beijing began restricting InP shipments in February 2025, and delays in export licenses have already rippled through the supply chain. Coherent, a Nvidia-backed chipmaker, flagged shortages in May, and CEO Jim Anderson raised the issue directly with U.S. officials during Trump’s recent trip to China. With China producing about 70% of global indium, the restrictions give Beijing leverage similar to its earlier rare earth curbs.

The impact is showing up in price and availability. A 6-inch InP wafer has jumped 250% to around $5,000 since the controls started, and suppliers like AXT report a backlog of orders due to permit delays. Lumentum says it’s sold out through 2028 despite quadrupling output, while Taiwanese substrate makers have faced disruptions from the same permit issues. Nvidia has responded by investing $2 billion each into Coherent and Lumentum, and Marvell bought Celestial AI to secure photonics capacity, but analysts say InP is now one of several upstream constraints gating data center buildouts. U.S. officials have discussed the issue with China in Seoul and Washington, but China’s commerce ministry hasn’t commented publicly on easing the restrictions.

text:
Lots of people are talking about $AAOI right now, but there are much better bets.

Here are 3 stocks that will perform way better imo:

1) $OPTX - optics/photonics components play.

2) $ASYS - semiconductor equipment and advanced packaging play.

3) $SHMD - advanced semiconductor packaging and substrate equipment play.

Here are the theses:

For a crazy run up, we need a company that checks all these boxes (credit goes to @mkfilko for making this criteria):

0) Enabler: Does this company build the foundation of the AI buildout?
1) Leadership: Is the founder experienced within this niche?
2) Revenue Quality: Is it recurring revenue, or a onetime sum.
3) Revenue Growth: Is there a thesis for an inflection point, or just steady growth.
4) Moat: Is it something only this company can do (at least without a lot of effort + time + money)?
5) Asymmetry: What's the worst-case scenario vs the possible reward?
6) Conviction Gap: How big is the space between what I can prove today and what the next catalysts will prove.

Now let's dive deep.

For $OPTX, this is what we got:

They are an enabler: AI data-center optics (micro-optic splitters/combiners, light pipes for hyperscale connectors) + LEO satellite optics that feed AI infrastructure. ☑️

Leadership: Founder-led. CEO Al Kapoor (Chairman & CEO) built the company from scratch in 1999 and owns 76-83% of the shares. Massive skin in the game. ☑️

Revenue Quality: Custom optics production (recurring once qualified). End-markets: defense (govt-stable), biomedical, AI data centers, space/LEO. Recent $21M+ capital raise cleaned up balance sheet (repaid revolver). Smaller absolute revenue but high-quality ramps. ☑️

Revenue Growth: 2025 revenue is $28M (flat but bottomed imo). Q1 2026 $6.5M (temporary biomedical shipment timing dip), but Q2 guidance >$7.5M (sequential rebound) and Q3 “strengthen further.” Explicit ramps in AI data-center optics, new defense programs, record space/LEO production. Design-stage programs converting to production.☑️

Moat: Vertically integrated custom photonics manufacturing (nano-machining, thin films, polymer/glass optics) in the Rochester optics hub. Mission-critical, high-precision components for defense/hyperscale AI. Hard switching costs once qualified.☑️

Asymmetry: $430-480M market cap on $28M revenue, but with 2026 ramps in AI/defense/space. Already ran hard (hundreds of percent YTD) yet founder ownership means low float and high conviction. Cash raised; debt cleaned. ☑️

Conviction Gap: Gap = pace of AI optics + defense/space ramps converting to revenue. Catalysts: Q2/Q3 sequential beats, new contract announcements, hyperscale production scale-up. Recent capital raise de-risks execution.☑️

For $ASYS we also check all the boxes: 

They are an enabler: Reflow ovens, diffusion furnaces, and polishing systems go into AI chip packaging/advanced substrates. AI revenue already 30-35% of Thermal Processing Solutions (TPS) and climbing toward 40%+ in Q3.☑️

Leadership: CEO Bob Daigle (Chairman/CEO) has deep semi-equipment experience. Not founder-led (company dates to 1981), but insiders own 27-34% and are buying aggressively: Director Robert Averick dropped $236k in May 2025 (and $275k earlier) at prices above the then-current level. No selling. New CFO just onboarded cleanly too.☑️

Revenue Quality: Mix of capital equipment + >20% recurring parts/services. Customer base diversified across semiconductor/device/assembly manufacturers. Geo mix includes US + Asia/Europe (some China exposure but not dominant). Cash flow turning positive with profitability; not dependent on one massive "whale" at all.☑️

Revenue Growth: Clear AI-driven inflection. Q2 FY2026 revenue $20.5M (+31% YoY, +8% QoQ), bookings $21.1M, backlog $22.3M. AI expected to drive majority of growth; Q3 guidance $20.5-22.5M. The capex is already supporting demand; next-gen high-density packaging equipment launching soon.☑️

Moat: Specialized thermal-processing equipment with long qualification cycles in semiconductors. Established brands (BTU International, Entrepix, PR Hoffman). Hyperscaler-driven AI packaging demand validates it indirectly.☑️

Asymmetry: $320-330M market cap on $80M+ run-rate revenue that is growing 30%+ with AI tailwinds. Cash is around $24M. Forward earnings/revenues look cheap vs. AI peers too imo.☑️

Conviction Gap: Gap = exact AI % trajectory and sustained margin leverage. Catalysts: Q3 earnings (AI >40% of TPS), SEMICON Taiwan launch, continued bookings strength. Evidence already leaning right (31% growth).☑️

And lastly, for $SHMD, I'm more bullish than ever:

Enabler: Also very strong. $SHMD provides specialized equipment and solutions that directly support AI chip packaging, advanced substrates, and high-performance semiconductor manufacturing. Its core technologies (thermal processing, polishing, and related systems) are essential for the high-density interconnects and thermal management required in AI accelerators. AI-related revenue is already a meaningful and growing portion of the mix and is expected to drive the majority of incremental growth.☑️

Leadership: Solid and aligned. The team has deep domain experience in semiconductor equipment and advanced packaging. Insiders and directors have shown clear skin in the game through recent purchases at prices above then-current levels, with no notable selling. The board and management appear focused on executing the AI-driven inflection rather than short-term optics.☑️

Revenue Quality: High quality with good visibility. A healthy mix of capital equipment sales plus recurring revenue from parts, services, and consumables (typically >20% of total). Customer base is diversified across leading semiconductor manufacturers, OSATs, and device makers. Geographic exposure is balanced (US, Asia, Europe) with manageable China concentration. Cash flow is turning positive as utilization rises, and the business is not overly dependent on a single “whale” customer.☑️

Forward Growth / Inflection: Clear and accelerating. Recent quarters have shown strong double-digit YoY growth driven by AI packaging demand. Bookings and backlog remain healthy, with new-generation high-density packaging equipment launching and expected to contribute meaningfully in the coming quarters. Guidance points to continued momentum, with AI expected to push overall growth rates higher as capacity comes online and design wins convert.☑️

Moat: Respectable. Long qualification cycles in the semiconductor supply chain create stickiness. Established process know-how and technology in thermal and polishing systems for advanced nodes give it an edge. Evidence is already tilting positively with recent growth and backlog trends. Near-term catalysts include upcoming quarterly results (showing continued AI momentum), new product launches at industry events (e.g., SEMICON), and further design-win conversions. The setup looks asymmetric if AI packaging demand continues to outpace legacy segments.☑️

$OPTX retested its ath and is now breaking out (I'm aiming for 22 on the LTF).

$ASYS is breaking out of a 30 years support/resistance zone (while consolidating this whole time). I'm aiming for upper 60s.

Just my 2 cents on what happens next.

But I believe this will be some of the best bets in the market here, even more than $AAOI.

text:
Opened a small position in $META. Looking into OTM leaps. 

$AMZN Bought OTM leap. Hoping for some consolidation around $240 / 245'ish. 

$CRDO decent price action on this name thus far. 
$AAOI so far I've only traded it ST twice 170+ to $200+ rinse & repeat. Options look bullish, 

 I like the sector going into end of year so any name in CPO's photonics would be a good choice. I'll personally be a bit more patient while building out positions as the MAG7 have shown some weakness lately and retesting some levels that would give us some indication about the next few months.

text:
$ASYS vs $VECO 

Which one's better?

Here's a review:

$ASYS just printed 31% y/y revenue growth in its latest quarter, expanded gross margins to 47.7% on the shift to higher-ASP AI packaging products, and moved solidly back into profitability. It trades at roughly 4x sales.

$VECO trades at a similar 3.7-4x sales. It is larger ($2.4B market cap vs $320M), more established, and carries exposure to the hot silicon photonics wave for AI optical interconnects. Both operate in the advanced packaging and process equipment layer that enables AI GPUs, chiplets, and photonics.

So why nearly identical multiples when one is smaller, growing faster in the core AI segment right now, and printing much higher gross margins?

Let's walk through it.

$ASYS specializes in thermal processing solutions: precision reflow ovens, belt furnaces, and related tools that handle the critical solder reflow and thermal steps in advanced semiconductor packaging for AI GPUs, chiplets, and high-power devices. This is the specialized “assembly oven” layer that enables high-yield, high-throughput packaging of complex multi-die architectures.

$VECO offers a broader set of process tools: wet processing/clean/etch platforms (recently qualified for new advanced packaging applications), laser spike annealing, ion beam deposition, plus MOCVD and tools supporting silicon photonics fabrication. Complementary steps in the packaging and photonics manufacturing flow.

They are not direct one-for-one competitors on every product, but they sit adjacent in the advanced packaging equipment supply chain for AI. Leading OSATs and IDMs building next-gen AI infrastructure often use tools from both (plus others) to complete the packaging and optical engine builds. You need robust thermal/reflow capability as part of the flows that VECO’s wet/anneal tools support in other steps.

In fact, strong AI packaging demand is pulling on the entire equipment ecosystem.

Here is why the market prices them so closely:

$VECO is the bigger, more diversified platform with scale across semiconductor, silicon photonics (AI optical I/O), data storage recovery, and scientific markets. More institutional coverage, better liquidity, and a “portfolio” story that captures multiple AI infrastructure vectors.

$ASYS is the smaller, more concentrated pure-play on the thermal/reflow step specifically for AI advanced packaging. Cleaner narrative, less exposed to cyclical data storage, but it carries the natural microcap liquidity and coverage discount.

$ASYS has already executed the mix shift. AI-related revenue is now >30% of its Thermal Processing segment and expected to exceed 40% soon, driving gross margins from deeply negative territory last year to 47%+ today with high incremental margins as volume scales.

$ASYS runs an asset-light model with focused subsidiaries and recently raised capital to accelerate growth and M&A. Order momentum and backlog are building on AI packaging demand.

That explains a lot of the similarity in multiples. But here is what $ASYS brings that creates the opportunity:

- Superior margin profile and operating leverage right now. The AI packaging reflow products carry high ASPs and high gross margins. As that mix climbs further, margins expand with limited opex growth.

- More direct, leveraged exposure to the AI GPU/chiplet packaging capex wave. This is one of the most capacity-constrained and critical parts of the AI buildout. Advanced thermal processes are essential for yield and reliability in high-density, high-power packages.

- Smaller base + re-rating torque. Proving sustained growth and 45%+ gross margins in a high-demand niche can support a move to 6-8x sales (still reasonable for this profile). That would be substantial upside from current levels. VECO already prices in more of its scale and photonics breadth.

- Clean U.S.-based positioning serving leading global packaging houses ramping AI products, with no China manufacturing overhang that affects some other names in the broader chain.

The takeaway and my thoughts:

I am not saying $ASYS deserves a massive premium to $VECO (but I do think this is the case lol). Scale, diversification (including photonics), and platform breadth at VECO justify a similar multiple.

But two names in the AI advanced packaging/photonics equipment ecosystem, both around 4x sales, where the smaller one is growing faster in its core AI segment, has dramatically higher and still-expanding gross margins, a cleaner focused story, and more asymmetric upside from here… that is worth sitting with.

$ASYS also has a more concentrated bet on the exact packaging equipment step seeing intense pull from every major AI accelerator designer moving to chiplet architectures.

What do you think?

Do you think $ASYS offers better risk/reward than $VECO (the larger peer in the same niche)?

I certainly do.

$ASYS is also breaking out of a 30 year support/resistance channel looking to breakout and use this as support.

$VECO already had its breakout (still seems like it wants to go up, just not as much as $ASYS here).

That's why I am long $ASYS.

If you guys got any other tickers that you want me to compare to each other, send them down below.

Here's a review: $ASYS just printed 31% y/y revenue growth in its latest quarter, expanded gross margins to 47.7% on the shift to higher-ASP AI packaging products, and moved solidly back into profitability. It trades at roughly 4x sales. $VECO trades at a similar 3.7-4x sales. It is larger ($2.4B market cap vs $320M), more established, and carries exposure to the hot silicon photonics wave for AI optical interconnects. Both operate in the advanced packaging and process equipment layer that enables AI GPUs, chiplets, and photonics. So why nearly identical multiples when one is smaller, growing faster in the core AI segment right now, and printing much higher gross margins? Let's walk through it. $ASYS specializes in thermal processing solutions: precision reflow ovens, belt furnaces, and related tools that handle the critical solder reflow and thermal steps in advanced semiconductor packaging for AI GPUs, chiplets, and high-power devices. This is the specialized “assembly oven” layer that enables high-yield, high-throughput packaging of complex multi-die architectures. $VECO offers a broader set of process tools: wet processing/clean/etch platforms (recently qualified for new advanced packaging applications), laser spike annealing, ion beam deposition, plus MOCVD and tools supporting silicon photonics fabrication. Complementary steps in the packaging and photonics manufacturing flow. They are not direct one-for-one competitors on every product, but they sit adjacent in the advanced packaging equipment supply chain for AI. Leading OSATs and IDMs building next-gen AI infrastructure often use tools from both (plus others) to complete the packaging and optical engine builds. You need robust thermal/reflow capability as part of the flows that VECO’s wet/anneal tools support in other steps. In fact, strong AI packaging demand is pulling on the entire equipment ecosystem. Here is why the market prices them so closely: $VECO is the bigger, more diversified platform with scale across semiconductor, silicon photonics (AI optical I/O), data storage recovery, and scientific markets. More institutional coverage, better liquidity, and a “portfolio” story that captures multiple AI infrastructure vectors. $ASYS is the smaller, more concentrated pure-play on the thermal/reflow step specifically for AI advanced packaging. Cleaner narrative, less exposed to cyclical data storage, but it carries the natural microcap liquidity and coverage discount. $ASYS has already executed the mix shift. AI-related revenue is now >30% of its Thermal Processing segment and expected to exceed 40% soon, driving gross margins from deeply negative territory last year to 47%+ today with high incremental margins as volume scales. $ASYS runs an asset-light model with focused subsidiaries and recently raised capital to accelerate growth and M&A. Order momentum and backlog are building on AI packaging demand. That explains a lot of the similarity in multiples. But here is what $ASYS brings that creates the opportunity: - Superior margin profile and operating leverage right now. The AI packaging reflow products carry high ASPs and high gross margins. As that mix climbs further, margins expand with limited opex growth. - More direct, leveraged exposure to the AI GPU/chiplet packaging capex wave. This is one of the most capacity-constrained and critical parts of the AI buildout. Advanced thermal processes are essential for yield and reliability in high-density, high-power packages. - Smaller base + re-rating torque. Proving sustained growth and 45%+ gross margins in a high-demand niche can support a move to 6-8x sales (still reasonable for this profile). That would be substantial upside from current levels. VECO already prices in more of its scale and photonics breadth. - Clean U.S.-based positioning serving leading global packaging houses ramping AI products, with no China manufacturing overhang that affects some other names in the broader chain. The takeaway and my thoughts: I am not saying $ASYS deserves a massive premium to $VECO (but I do think this is the case lol). Scale, diversification (including photonics), and platform breadth at VECO justify a similar multiple. But two names in the AI advanced packaging/photonics equipment ecosystem, both around 4x sales, where the smaller one is growing faster in its core AI segment, has dramatically higher and still-expanding gross margins, a cleaner focused story, and more asymmetric upside from here… that is worth sitting with. $ASYS also has a more concentrated bet on the exact packaging equipment step seeing intense pull from every major AI accelerator designer moving to chiplet architectures. What do you think? Do you think $ASYS offers better risk/reward than $VECO (the larger peer in the same niche)? I certainly do. $ASYS is also breaking out of a 30 year support/resistance channel looking to breakout and use this as support. $VECO already had its breakout (still seems like it wants to go up, just not as much as $ASYS here). That's why I am long $ASYS. If you guys got any other tickers that you want me to compare to each other, send them down below."}}}, "rest_id": "2064067131327877327", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "3560", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
$AAOI - Zoom out to the prize

How fast is the CPO market $AAOI feeds actually growing?

Per Yole Group, the most respected analyst in photonics, pure co-packaged optics scales from ~$46M (2024) to ~$8.1B (2030). 

That’s a 137% CAGR - one of the steepest curves anywhere in AI infrastructure (~176× in six years).

A vertically integrated laser/light-source supplier sitting dead center of that ramp is exactly the kind of name you want to own early. 

(Source: Yole Group — “Co-Packaged Optics for Data Centers 2025.”)

text:
MY PORTFOLIO EXPLAINED
I have been meticulously building a portfolio based on the thesis that the Physical AI revolution is upon us.
I have chosen each of these companies, both for what they do and for providing asymmetrical return opportunity to investors. 

$SIVE  Sivers Semiconductors 
Sivers makes high-frequency radio frequency (RF) millimeter-wave (mmWave) transceivers and advanced optical semiconductors through its Wireless and Photonics divisions. In the AI ecosystem, the company is a critical enabler of next-generation data centers, collaborating with foundry giants like GlobalFoundries to integrate its laser arrays into silicon photonics and co-packaged optics (CPO) platforms to eliminate interconnect bandwidth bottlenecks for massive workloads. Concurrently, its mmWave technologies supply high-speed wireless connectivity for satellite communications, 5G telecom, and edge infrastructure, providing the high-throughput network backbone necessary for real-time edge AI coordination and smart cities.  

$LPK LPKF Laser & Electronics 
LPKF makes high-precision, laser-based micro-material manufacturing systems, headlined by its proprietary Laser Induced Deep Etching (LIDE) technology. As conventional organic substrates struggle with heavy thermal loads and warpage from massive chiplet integration, LPKF provides the precise tools needed to drill defect-free through-glass vias (TGVs) at aspect ratios up to 1:50 with zero micro-cracks. This specialized capability directly underpins advanced hardware and high-performance computing, enabling leading chipmakers to scale up substrate sizes, integrate high-bandwidth memory (HBM), and implement in-package microfluidic cooling for the next wave of physical AI chips.  

$ACCON Acconeer
Acconeer makes ultra-low-power, miniature 60 GHz Pulsed Coherent Radar (PCR) sensors that fuse millimeter-level depth precision with an exceptionally small spatial footprint. Within Edge AI and robotics, Acconeer’s chips allow tiny, battery-constrained hardware to execute real-time motion tracking, presence detection, and material or surface classification without relying on heavy cloud computation or invasive optical cameras. By deploying deep learning interfaces that compile models directly onto its integrated microcontrollers, the company provides robots, drones, and consumer devices with the localized spatial awareness required for autonomous navigation, gesture control, and power-efficient smart presence sensing.  

$SILC  Silicom 
Silicom designs and manufactures high-performance networking and data infrastructure hardware, including server network interface cards (NICs), programmable FPGA-based acceleration cards, SmartNICs, and edge switches. The company anchors Edge AI and smart systems by offloading heavy encryption, data compression, and packet-routing tasks from host CPUs, allowing decentralized AI infrastructure to maintain ultra-low latency and maximum data throughput. Operating primarily via design wins with cybersecurity firms, hyperscalers, and telcos, Silicom enables the secure, low-latency processing fabrics required for distributed AI inference networks and 5G/6G open radio access network (O-RAN) rollouts.  

$GAPW-B Gapwaves
Gapwaves develops and fabricates specialized, millimeter-wave antennas utilizing its patented gap-waveguide technology. This low-loss, cost-efficient hardware is engineered to maximize the resolution, beam direction, and overall range of high-frequency radar systems without increasing power consumption. Gapwaves fits squarely into the autonomous systems and smart city landscapes by supplying Tier-1 automotive partners with the structural building blocks for next-generation ADAS (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems), enabling autonomous vehicles, industrial transport robots, and urban traffic monitors to build reliable, high-definition models of their surroundings under any weather condition.

$SEYE Smart Eye
Smart Eye makes computer-vision hardware and software platforms dedicated to Human Insight AI, tracking eye gaze, facial expressions, and head movements in real time. The company’s primary footprint is in the automotive sector, where its edge-based Driver Monitoring Systems (DMS) and interior cabin sensing modules identify operator distraction, drowsiness, and cognitive load to comply with global safety mandates. This technology bridges the human-machine interface across smart transportation and autonomous vehicles, while its subsidiaries (like Affectiva) extend this technology into emotion AI, which is increasingly utilized in behavioral research, aerospace simulators, and interactive robotics.

$AMBA Ambarella
Ambarella builds ultra-low-power, high-efficiency system-on-chip (SoC) vision and radar processors built on its algorithm-first CVflow artificial intelligence architecture. Its chips, like the 4nm CV7 and the high-compute N1 series, serve as the localized brain for Edge AI, industrial robotics, and smart city infrastructure by running complex convolutional and transformer neural networks simultaneously. By integrating advanced image signal processing, hardware-accelerated video encoding, and native neural network computation on a single die, Ambarella allows security cameras, aerial drones, and autonomous mobile robots (AMRs) to execute edge-based multi-sensor perception, visual odometry, and real-time SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) entirely disconnected from the cloud.

$OUST Ouster
 Ouster designs and manufactures high-resolution 3D digital LiDAR sensors alongside integrated perception suites, following its strategic combination with Stereolabs' ZED camera technology. By embedding native color and precise spatial mapping directly onto its proprietary silicon architecture, Ouster creates an end-to-end sensing platform for Physical AI. This hardware acts as the primary sensory organ for humanoid robotics, warehouse automation, autonomous mining fleets, and smart city infrastructure (via its BlueCity traffic management software), delivering low-latency, resilient environmental data that allows machines to map, track, and safely navigate complex physical spaces.

$AMZN Amazon
Within the AI and robotics verticals, Amazon develops custom infrastructure silicon—such as its proprietary Trainium and Inferentia acceleration chips—while engineering vast, automated logistics systems for its global fulfillment network. The company is a pioneer in warehouse robotics, deploying fleets of fully autonomous mobile robots like Proteus and intelligent manipulation systems like Sparrow to streamline its supply chain via machine learning and reinforcement learning. On the edge, Amazon implements localized AI and natural language processing through consumer ecosystems like Alexa, Ring, and the Astro home robot, establishing a ubiquitous presence across cloud AI architecture, industrial automation, and smart home systems.

$ELMT Elmet Group
Elmet Group fabricates precision-engineered components and high-power radio frequency (RF) or microwave equipment, operating through its Critical Materials Components and Engineered Microwave Products divisions. Specializing in the custom manufacturing of refractory metals like pure tungsten and molybdenum, Elmet supplies the foundational, high-durability hardware components required for advanced semiconductor fabrication, aerospace systems, and defense electronics. It fits into the advanced hardware and AI supply chain by delivering the critical thermal management substrates, ion implantation shielding, and robust RF distribution modules necessary to manufacture and run high-energy compute chips.

$TRT Trio-Tech International 
Trio-Tech provides semiconductor back-end testing services and manufactures heavy-duty reliability equipment, including customized burn-in boards, wet process stations, and environmental testing chambers. The company serves as a vital gatekeeper for advanced hardware reliability, validating that complex chips can withstand severe thermal and electrical stress without failing prior to commercial rollout. Driven by the massive ramp-up in next-generation computing architectures, Trio-Tech directly supports AI and smart automotive expansion by stress-testing high-performance GPUs, CPUs, and electric vehicle (EV) electronics across its key facilities in Southeast Asia.

$HLIT Harmonic
Harmonic produces virtualized broadband access networks and software-based cloud video delivery infrastructure, known for solutions like its VOS360 cloud platform and cOS applications. The company embeds AI into smart systems by leveraging Content-Aware Encoding (CAE) algorithms and dynamic ad-insertion technologies to process mass media streams at the network edge, squeezing bandwidth requirements by up to 50% without compromising visual quality. This positions Harmonic as a fundamental utility provider for edge systems and smart cities, optimizing the heavy data pipelines that distribute live television, ultra-high-definition streaming, and localized broadband connectivity at scale.

Within Edge AI and robotics, Acconeer’s chips allow tiny, battery-constrained hardware to execute real-time motion tracking, presence detection, and material or surface classification without relying on heavy cloud computation or invasive optical cameras. By deploying deep learning interfaces that compile models directly onto its integrated microcontrollers, the company provides robots, drones, and consumer devices with the localized spatial awareness required for autonomous navigation, gesture control, and power-efficient smart presence sensing.   $SILC Silicom Silicom designs and manufactures high-performance networking and data infrastructure hardware, including server network interface cards (NICs), programmable FPGA-based acceleration cards, SmartNICs, and edge switches. The company anchors Edge AI and smart systems by offloading heavy encryption, data compression, and packet-routing tasks from host CPUs, allowing decentralized AI infrastructure to maintain ultra-low latency and maximum data throughput. Operating primarily via design wins with cybersecurity firms, hyperscalers, and telcos, Silicom enables the secure, low-latency processing fabrics required for distributed AI inference networks and 5G/6G open radio access network (O-RAN) rollouts.   $GAPW-B Gapwaves Gapwaves develops and fabricates specialized, millimeter-wave antennas utilizing its patented gap-waveguide technology. This low-loss, cost-efficient hardware is engineered to maximize the resolution, beam direction, and overall range of high-frequency radar systems without increasing power consumption. Gapwaves fits squarely into the autonomous systems and smart city landscapes by supplying Tier-1 automotive partners with the structural building blocks for next-generation ADAS (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems), enabling autonomous vehicles, industrial transport robots, and urban traffic monitors to build reliable, high-definition models of their surroundings under any weather condition. $SEYE Smart Eye Smart Eye makes computer-vision hardware and software platforms dedicated to Human Insight AI, tracking eye gaze, facial expressions, and head movements in real time. The company’s primary footprint is in the automotive sector, where its edge-based Driver Monitoring Systems (DMS) and interior cabin sensing modules identify operator distraction, drowsiness, and cognitive load to comply with global safety mandates. This technology bridges the human-machine interface across smart transportation and autonomous vehicles, while its subsidiaries (like Affectiva) extend this technology into emotion AI, which is increasingly utilized in behavioral research, aerospace simulators, and interactive robotics. $AMBA Ambarella Ambarella builds ultra-low-power, high-efficiency system-on-chip (SoC) vision and radar processors built on its algorithm-first CVflow artificial intelligence architecture. Its chips, like the 4nm CV7 and the high-compute N1 series, serve as the localized brain for Edge AI, industrial robotics, and smart city infrastructure by running complex convolutional and transformer neural networks simultaneously. By integrating advanced image signal processing, hardware-accelerated video encoding, and native neural network computation on a single die, Ambarella allows security cameras, aerial drones, and autonomous mobile robots (AMRs) to execute edge-based multi-sensor perception, visual odometry, and real-time SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) entirely disconnected from the cloud. $OUST Ouster Ouster designs and manufactures high-resolution 3D digital LiDAR sensors alongside integrated perception suites, following its strategic combination with Stereolabs' ZED camera technology. By embedding native color and precise spatial mapping directly onto its proprietary silicon architecture, Ouster creates an end-to-end sensing platform for Physical AI. This hardware acts as the primary sensory organ for humanoid robotics, warehouse automation, autonomous mining fleets, and smart city infrastructure (via its BlueCity traffic management software), delivering low-latency, resilient environmental data that allows machines to map, track, and safely navigate complex physical spaces. $AMZN Amazon Within the AI and robotics verticals, Amazon develops custom infrastructure silicon—such as its proprietary Trainium and Inferentia acceleration chips—while engineering vast, automated logistics systems for its global fulfillment network. The company is a pioneer in warehouse robotics, deploying fleets of fully autonomous mobile robots like Proteus and intelligent manipulation systems like Sparrow to streamline its supply chain via machine learning and reinforcement learning. On the edge, Amazon implements localized AI and natural language processing through consumer ecosystems like Alexa, Ring, and the Astro home robot, establishing a ubiquitous presence across cloud AI architecture, industrial automation, and smart home systems. $ELMT Elmet Group Elmet Group fabricates precision-engineered components and high-power radio frequency (RF) or microwave equipment, operating through its Critical Materials Components and Engineered Microwave Products divisions. Specializing in the custom manufacturing of refractory metals like pure tungsten and molybdenum, Elmet supplies the foundational, high-durability hardware components required for advanced semiconductor fabrication, aerospace systems, and defense electronics. It fits into the advanced hardware and AI supply chain by delivering the critical thermal management substrates, ion implantation shielding, and robust RF distribution modules necessary to manufacture and run high-energy compute chips. $TRT Trio-Tech International Trio-Tech provides semiconductor back-end testing services and manufactures heavy-duty reliability equipment, including customized burn-in boards, wet process stations, and environmental testing chambers. The company serves as a vital gatekeeper for advanced hardware reliability, validating that complex chips can withstand severe thermal and electrical stress without failing prior to commercial rollout. Driven by the massive ramp-up in next-generation computing architectures, Trio-Tech directly supports AI and smart automotive expansion by stress-testing high-performance GPUs, CPUs, and electric vehicle (EV) electronics across its key facilities in Southeast Asia. $HLIT Harmonic Harmonic produces virtualized broadband access networks and software-based cloud video delivery infrastructure, known for solutions like its VOS360 cloud platform and cOS applications. The company embeds AI into smart systems by leveraging Content-Aware Encoding (CAE) algorithms and dynamic ad-insertion technologies to process mass media streams at the network edge, squeezing bandwidth requirements by up to 50% without compromising visual quality. This positions Harmonic as a fundamental utility provider for edge systems and smart cities, optimizing the heavy data pipelines that distribute live television, ultra-high-definition streaming, and localized broadband connectivity at scale."}}}, "rest_id": "2064063206776254486", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "14825", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Yeah… I think all your upstream semi supply chain companies are going much higher.

Goldman now expects a combined $5.3 trillion of capex spending for the four largest hyperscalers $GOOGL / $META / MSFT / $AMZN from 2025 to 2030.

Revised up from $4.5T from Q1 earnings.

“Aggregate capex est. $7.6 trillion between 2026 and 2031.”

And it flows upward to these tiny chokepoints like $SIVE for CPO lasers/ $SOI for Silicon Photonics substrates. Leaderdrive/Harmonic for Humanoids components.

And so on…

Ai names don’t move in a straight line up,
but is just the beginning of the next Industrial Revolution as we move from  R&D/compute buildout into commercialization from Agents -> Physical AI -> discovery.

text:
$SIVE is my favorite CPO / photonics stock after AAOI.

Partly because it's Swedish and you have entertainment from comedians over there.

Today a new non-technical hedge fund called Protean Funds (likely shorting), went on air.

To said $SIVE CPO applications are imaginary.

Right after $GFS just made $SIVE their reference laser.

(Just for some context to newer readers: Lot of people in Sweden can only look at past 12 month revenue, and don't understand concepts of forward growth)

Also because they don't understand that no CPO application has scaled up yet at all.

So Swedish hedge funds keep going short (with many of their hedge funds like Colosseum / Origo heavily underwater).

But... for the technical readers... from H2 2026 to 2028, it goes from near $0 to $91B TAM in 1 1/2 years. (we're entering H2 now).

Overall TAM hits $141B (which is also 10x+ or so in 1 1/2 years)... and $SIVE has scaled into pluggable market with $JBL + other unnamed pluggable players with that too.

Probably not going to end well for the local Swedish firms, shorting right before the largest inflection points ever hits for $SIVE.

Just a matter of time before volume ramps.

text:
Okay... just some more weekend shower thoughts about $XFAB.

I still feel like it could be the next $TSEM, just early stage at a $1.4B MC?

They kinda leapfrogged current gens (which $TSEM are getting volume from) to compete for H2 2027 CPO scale up inflection point ($ASX docs cite Xfab (aka. photonixFAB) as focusing on CPO)

By building out some black magic MTP (transfer printing) architecture for lasers w/ other stuff like TFLN.

Basically next-gen integration IP, they're still behind on yields, sure.

But $NVDA evaluating it for transceivers/switches to see if it can volume ramp. That $NOK sets the specifications/assembly for. (nvidia invested in nokia for this these switches/networking too btw).

And if their MTP supply chain works... (eg. with Smartphotonics providing lasers, EU players doing assembly).

It basically volume ramps with $NVDA just like why Nvidia signed long term agreements with $TSEM?

Downside risk?

Already below replacement book value, can always go lower yeah, but typically to a certain point.

Maybe more CHIPS act subsidies next few months from chips act 2. If it doesn't go well there's SiC (152% Y/Y Growth, 195% Y/Y SiC wafer shipment growth)/GaN power semi upside.

Europeans /LLMs will say "oh evaluations doesn't mean it's a future contract!".

This is kinda different since the European Union is behind this effort and $XFAB for soverign photonic supply chains.

Not your typical company + hyperscaler evaluation, since $NVDA wants to be nice to Europe's regulators. They'd prob be pissed if nvidia just stayed in US/Taiwan/China.

So if they can make this MTP black magic work with mass production, feels almost for sure nvidia/nokia volume ramp on some tiny $1.4B silicon photonics foundry or at least throw them a bone with smaller contracts.

In terms of timelines, maybe just a months early since it volume ramps H2 2027/H1 2028 (which happens to be in line with CPO scale up timelines)...

Or just unknown because they named their project something stupid like photonixfab?

Like XFAB Photonics would have been better? so institutions/screeners can connect the dots when looking at CPO silicon photonic foundry players?

Automotive should also coming out of a slump medium term, sped up by self-driving (TSM Chairmain comments yesterday said ai automotive was TSM's growth vector alongside robotics). So their core business also should pick up speed too medium term.

Obviously markets/europeans want a "Nvidia signs $2B+ contract, XFab volume ramping 2027!"

But by then it will be a $9B+ company and you miss out on all the upside. And especially since everyone analyst/institution is blind to volume expectations for these....

Normally don't invest in companies in evaluation stages, but this just seems very de-risked by EU sovereignty + Gov backing, and you have Nvidia + Nokia there for volumes if they can make the IP work.

I think markets are probably missing something here...  there's almost 0 value being assigned to being CPO exposure in Europe as their long term upside.

text: @Qlarividencia @halldj00 They're 'Fab-Lite' not fabless. Fabs (WIN/GF) only make the raw wafers. Sivers owns the IP/InP laser recipe, so they must fund tech transfer, specialized tooling, & proprietary testing/back-end packing (where 80% of photonics cost sits) to scale massive AI volume.

text:
We're excited to be at CYTO 2026, connecting with the flow cytometry community and highlighting Hypereon™, Hamamatsu's next-generation photodetector technology. Built on Hamamatsu's decades of expertise in PMTs and APDs, Hypereon™ combines two stages of amplification—electron bombardment and on-chip APD gain—to deliver the sensitivity, dynamic range, temperature stability, and sensor-to-sensor uniformity demanded by today's most advanced flow cytometry systems. When paired with our Photon Number Quantification (PNQ) algorithm, Hypereon™ helps transform qualitative measurements into quantitative, comparable data.

The conference has already sparked valuable conversations with researchers, instrument manufacturers, and industry partners about emerging trends and the future of flow cytometry. We look forward to continuing these discussions over the next two days and exploring new opportunities to advance life science research together.

If you're attending CYTO 2026, stop by booth 228 to meet the team and learn more about Hypereon™ and Hamamatsu's photonics solutions for next-generation flow cytometry systems.

Learn more: https://t.co/Ztb6E26h94

Built on Hamamatsu's decades of expertise in PMTs and APDs, Hypereon™ combines two stages of amplification—electron bombardment and on-chip APD gain—to deliver the sensitivity, dynamic range, temperature stability, and sensor-to-sensor uniformity demanded by today's most advanced flow cytometry systems. When paired with our Photon Number Quantification (PNQ) algorithm, Hypereon™ helps transform qualitative measurements into quantitative, comparable data. The conference has already sparked valuable conversations with researchers, instrument manufacturers, and industry partners about emerging trends and the future of flow cytometry. We look forward to continuing these discussions over the next two days and exploring new opportunities to advance life science research together. If you're attending CYTO 2026, stop by booth 228 to meet the team and learn more about Hypereon™ and Hamamatsu's photonics solutions for next-generation flow cytometry systems. text:
$STM - the 2027 ramp is my thesis

Forget the YTD chart. 
The question that matters: does the earnings ramp show up?

→ Data center revenue guided to ~$1B in 2026, then doubling again toward ~$2B in 2027 (company guidance, echoed by BofA on its PT raise to $83). 

That’s the fastest-growing line in the model.

→ The driver is silicon photonics + power chips into AI infrastructure. 

$STM is weighing a Crolles fab expansion by end-2026 specifically because photonics demand is accelerating. 

Capacity decisions = conviction signal.

→ $SPCX selected the STM32V8 for Starlink’s inter-satellite laser links. 

Broader OEM availability follows 2026. 

Design wins now ⇒ revenue later.

→ The real unlock is operating leverage. Net margin is a depressed 1.4% TTM with auto/industrial at trough. 

As the cyclical floor lifts and high-margin data-center mix scales, EPS ramps off a low base.

→ FCF inflection backs it: TTM FCF ≈ -$700M, projected to swing to ~+$967M in 2026 and build toward ~$3.5B by 2030 (Simply Wall St estimates). 

Cash generation is the proof, not the promise.

I am betting that data-center compounding plus an auto/industrial trough recovery converges in 2027. 

If both hit, the multiple re-rates on rising earnings instead of sentiment.

Key watch item = next print’s data-center run-rate. 

That’s the tell on whether the doubling is on track.

Excited to see this one play out - quite sure @damnang2 is also long on this one 🔥

text:
9/ My Take/Risks

SIVE isn’t a stock for investors looking for certainty.

It’s a stock for investors willing to accept significant risk in exchange for potential upside if AI networking, silicon photonics and optical infrastructure continue to grow.

Risks:

❌ Not profitable
❌ Highly speculative
❌ Extremely volatile
❌ Commercial adoption still needs to materialise

With that in mind, I’m building a position which will make up no more than 2% of my portfolio. This is at about 1% currently. I have some cash allocated to buy dips and will continue to do so alongside other investments.

text:
5/ Silicon Photonics

Sivers is designing its laser technology to integrate into silicon photonics ecosystems.

Rather than simply selling standalone components, the goal is to become part of the wider infrastructure stack.

Silicon photonics replaces electrical data transfer with light inside chips, massively improving bandwidth and energy efficiency — and Sivers is focused on providing the external laser systems that power these photonic networks.

text:
Sequoia’s @shaunmmaguire wrote a private hardware manifesto arguing that over the next 25 years, most of the money will be made in hardware:

"Every software revolution is preceded by a hardware revolution."

"To have the iOS App Store that enabled Uber, DoorDash, and all of these great companies - you needed to have the iPhone."

"This AI revolution - we're seeing what it can do from the software layer, but it's still limited by hardware."

"The hardware we were doing for a long time was all following Moore's Law. It was all branching out of this decision in the mid-1950s to go all in on the silicon supply chain."

"That has created magic, and there's still a couple orders of magnitude of juice to squeeze, but we’re hitting fundamental physics limits - Dennard scaling, things like that."

"I think this tech tree is branching into humanoid robots, into silicon photonics, into orbital data centers - all of these new hardware areas where there's going to be 20+ years of progress."

"There's going to be incredible businesses built on the back of this. And a lot of dumpster fires."

From his appearance on the show in March.

Coherent is a technology leader in lasers, optical components, and semiconductor materials, with its products widely used in industrial, telecommunications, and EV applications. Its strengths lie in its high-barrier technologies and innovative capabilities. The stock saw a significant gain today, benefiting from the recovery in the technology manufacturing sector.

Future trends are strong: growing demand for 5G/6G, fiber-optic communications, and laser processing will drive orders. The expansion of AI-related applications further opens up growth opportunities, with high certainty of growth in the medium to long term.

text:
Seeing a lot of hype around $VECO with nobody discussing or realizing the actual risk behind it.  

If you're running into VECO because your favorite Furu told you to without ever mentioning the word "SAMR", think twice. 

$VECO has one gatekeeper left between it and its merger: China's State Administration for Market Regulation (SAMR).

The market is treating SAMR like paperwork. It isn't. It's the most quietly weaponized regulatory desk in the US-China conflict, and right now it's holding this deal hostage.

Start with why Beijing even gets a vote on two American companies merging. SAMR jurisdiction attaches because Veeco and Axcelis both sell into China above the revenue thresholds - their own China business is the leash. 

The deal needs Beijing's blessing precisely because both companies need Beijing's market.

And SAMR's signature move was never the formal block. It's the pocket veto. 

Look up what happened to the Qualcomm/NXP deal. It never got rejected, it just bled out over years of stalling. 

Intel/Tower, same playbook five years later. Silence past the outside date, years of stalling, and another dead deal.  

That's what makes this binary nearly impossible to price. A rejection would at least be information. Silence is just time passing, and markets are terrible at pricing a veto disguised as a calendar - every quiet month reads as "still on track," right up until the deadline does the executing itself.

Here's the aggravating detail specific to Veeco: while it waits on Beijing's approval, WASHINGTON is restricting what Veeco may sell to Beijing. 

The United States BIS license requirements just carved $8M out of Q1 and stranded ~$15M of annealing systems at port. 

Veeco is asking a favor from the very government its own government is squeezing.

Put yourself behind SAMR's desk this summer. Export controls tightening, minerals leverage being traded openly, a Gulf crisis repricing every strategic supply chain (and across your blotter sits a pending US semicap merger: unilateral, costless, completely deniable leverage).

Why would you spend that chip a day earlier than you had to?

Even approval has a trap inside it. When SAMR does clear American tech deals, it increasingly attaches supply-continuity conditions to Chinese customers. So imagine NewCo legally bound by Beijing to keep supplying the same customers Washington requires licenses to ship to. Two regulators, incompatible orders, one company as the rope.

Map the tree honestly and it gets worse. Clean approval is largely priced after a 25% post-earnings run. Conditioned approval hands NewCo a compliance contradiction nobody has modeled. And silence past the outside date leaves you holding standalone VECO at ~35x guided EPS against an analyst deck still parked in the $30s. Two of the three branches hurt, and the good one pays the least.

What fintwit keeps missing is that the whole timeline is exogenous. Management can't accelerate it, lobby it, or guide it - "second half 2026" is an aspiration about another sovereign's negotiating calendar. You aren't underwriting a semiconductor company here. You're underwriting the temperature of US-China relations through December.

None of this says Veeco is a bad company. The orders are real, the photonics franchise is real. It's just that says the next six months don't belong to the company at all. 

They belong to a regulator in Beijing with a costless veto and a calendar for a weapon.

Great business (I actually think if not for this SAMR situation it would be an immediate buy), but until this is resolved, hard pass. 

NFA.

text:
🚨 NEW TRADES ALERT

Rep. Gil Cisneros just disclosed up to $2,500,000 worth of new trades.

Cisneros sits on House Committee on Armed Services, a Navy veteran and former Under Secretary of Defense.

His latest disclosure includes buying the following military/defense related stocks within 3 weeks (May 4 - 29):

$THLEF - French defense electronics company, major NATO contractor in radars, missiles, naval combat systems, and satellite comms.

$DCO - U.S. aerospace & defense components supplier, sells to Boeing, Lockheed, Raytheon and more.

$SARO - Aerospace engine MRO, including military engine overhauls for the U.S. Air Force and allied air forces.

$CRWD - Major federal cybersecurity contracts including Department of Defense.

$AMD - Semis with growing Department of Defense AI/compute contracts.

$NOVT - Precision photonics including defense laser applications.

$TEL - Connectors widely used in military electronics.

$ST - Sensors with defense/aerospace applications.

$CIEN - Optical networking, has federal/defense customers.

$FN - Optical components manufacturer, defense customer base.

$TOELF - Indirect defense tie via the chip supply chain.

text:
Most people think the AI buildout is just a couple of stocks – Memory, CPU, GPU, NeoClouds, Photonics..

$SNDK, $MU, $ARM $INTC, $AMD, $NBIS, $LITE, $AAOI, $TSEM, $SIVE

The reality is there’s so much more to it.. I built a complete map of the AI buildout – at least the one I’ve been trading around for the last year.. I put that map on my Substack… 12 layers deep.

Since I’ve seen it circulating on here, I’ll just post it myself.

The hard part isn’t knowing the full stack – it’s making sense of it.

Why is photonics down today but memory isn’t? Why is CPO so relevant in the AI buildout?

Bullish AF on AI infrastructure.

Go check out the full article for free on my Substack, and if you’re going to share it – please do, but link back to my article.

Here is the link: https://t.co/AqKTdxnlmJ

Let’s go find the next 1000% stock ⚡️⚡️

text:
📢 𝐉𝐔𝐒𝐓 𝐈𝐍: $MU Micron Appoints Former NVIDIA Executive Alexis Black Björlin to Board - $NVDA $META $AVGO $INTC 

👉 𝐊𝐞𝐲 𝐇𝐢𝐠𝐡𝐥𝐢𝐠𝐡𝐭𝐬:

➤  Micron appoints Dr. 𝐀𝐥𝐞𝐱𝐢𝐬 𝐁𝐥𝐚𝐜𝐤 𝐁𝐣ö𝐫𝐥𝐢𝐧 to its board of directors.

➤  Björlin currently serves as 𝐂𝐡𝐢𝐞𝐟 𝐒𝐭𝐫𝐚𝐭𝐞𝐠𝐲 𝐎𝐟𝐟𝐢𝐜𝐞𝐫 at General Catalyst.

➤  Previously led 𝐃𝐆𝐗 𝐂𝐥𝐨𝐮𝐝 as senior vice president at NVIDIA.

➤  Brings expertise across 𝐀𝐈 𝐢𝐧𝐟𝐫𝐚𝐬𝐭𝐫𝐮𝐜𝐭𝐮𝐫𝐞, cloud, semiconductors, and networking.

➤  Held senior leadership roles at 𝐌𝐞𝐭𝐚, 𝐁𝐫𝐨𝐚𝐝𝐜𝐨𝐦, 𝐈𝐧𝐭𝐞𝐥, and Source Photonics.

➤  Serves on boards including 𝐃𝐢𝐠𝐢𝐭𝐚𝐥 𝐑𝐞𝐚𝐥𝐭𝐲, Astera Labs, and Celestial AI.

💬 𝐄𝐱𝐩𝐞𝐫𝐭 𝐒𝐭𝐚𝐭𝐞𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭:

“Alexis is a respected technology and business leader with exceptional expertise at the intersection of AI infrastructure, cloud systems and semiconductors,” said Sanjay Mehrotra, Chairman, President and CEO of Micron.

“Her experience leading large-scale technology platforms and advancing some of the industry’s most important innovations will be a tremendous asset to Micron. We are very pleased to welcome Alexis to our board and look forward to the insight she will bring as we continue to build on our technology leadership and capture the significant opportunities ahead.”

“I am delighted to welcome Alexis to the Micron board of directors,” said Lynn Dugle, Micron’s lead independent director.

“Alexis brings a rare combination of technical depth, strategic vision and operating experience across some of the world’s most important technology companies. I am confident she will contribute to our board’s work and to Micron’s long-term success.”

credit first: this one's real. a named, booked $8.2M production order (https://t.co/pcEEAe5HxP, Ka-band beamforming ICs) beats the "reference design / photonixFAB option" stuff this name usually trades on. take the win.
but the next line is the tell: "the bigger implication is not the contract size." when someone tells you to look past the number and feel the implication, they're selling the sequel, not the order. one $8.2M order isn't a defense-prime design-in that "leads to volume contracts" yet — that's the hoped-for next chapter written as done. and now it's a space/defense play and a SpaceX-IPO play and the AI-photonics story, all stacked on one micro-cap.
price the $8.2M that exists, not the ramp that's still a sentence. watch the filings for repeat orders. NFA. $SIVE

text:
Marvell as the safest entry into the photonics buildout. Ouster + FieldAI for color lidar on mobile robots. Sivers' GF deal for 1.6-terabit lasers (the stock is up around 1,700% YTD).

That's @kaizen_investor's thesis, tracked on schwerelos. $MRVL +148%, OUST +178%, SIVE +185% YTD on the follow-returns. 30 scored calls, 66.67% win rate, #2 on the board.

4 stocks up 10%+ today in a sea of red that I’m bullish on long-term.

$BESS $VECO $FCEL $ADUR

Do your own DD, but I think all 4 attack unique bottlenecks most investors aren’t paying attention to.

Repost. Bookmark. Subscribe. Thank you all for the support. 

$BESS

Thinking out loud…

Everyone is focused on GPUs, but AI doesn’t work without power. Bimergen is developing roughly 2 GW across 23 battery storage projects and has talked about a path to $300M-$400M in annual revenue over the next 3-4 years. They recently advanced a 100 MW / 400 MWh Redbird project, acquired 79.2 MW of late-stage ERCOT assets, and partnered on an additional 480 MWh portfolio.

The niche here is grid storage. AI data centers are creating enormous electricity demand and storage is becoming increasingly important for grid stability, renewable integration, and power availability.

High risk, but the valuation disconnect is interesting.

$VECO

This might be my favorite of the group.

One of the biggest AI bottlenecks isn’t compute anymore—it’s moving data efficiently. Veeco recently announced more than $250M in equipment orders tied to Indium Phosphide laser production for 800G and 1.6T optical transceivers used in AI infrastructure.

FY26 revenue guidance is $740M-$800M and management expects compound semiconductor revenue to grow roughly 50% this year.

The niche is optical connectivity. AI clusters need photonics, silicon photonics, and advanced networking to connect thousands of accelerators together. That’s where Veeco’s equipment comes in.

$FCEL

The earnings numbers weren’t pretty.

Q2 revenue came in at $35.6M and the company posted a loss, but what caught my attention was the pipeline.

Management reported a 4 GW sales pipeline, up 267% sequentially, with roughly 90% tied to data center opportunities. They also launched a standardized 12.5 MW Energy Block and are expanding manufacturing capacity toward 500 MW annually.

The niche is data center power generation. Grid interconnection delays are becoming a major AI bottleneck and companies are looking for reliable behind-the-meter power solutions.

Risky, but definitely worth watching.

$ADUR

This one isn’t directly AI related, but it solves a different infrastructure bottleneck.

Aduro recently reported a 47-hour continuous pilot run with 86% liquid hydrocarbon yields and about 85% of output in the C20-and-below range suitable for existing naphtha cracker infrastructure.

Cash sits around $39M and the company is advancing its first commercial-scale facility in Europe.

The niche is industrial efficiency and feedstock processing. If successful, the technology could unlock value from waste streams that traditional recycling struggles to handle while integrating into existing petrochemical infrastructure.

Not every opportunity has to be AI. Sometimes the biggest winners solve overlooked bottlenecks in large industries.

Just some names I’m watching closely.

Repost. Bookmark. Subscribe. Thank you all for the support. $BESS Thinking out loud… Everyone is focused on GPUs, but AI doesn’t work without power. Bimergen is developing roughly 2 GW across 23 battery storage projects and has talked about a path to $300M-$400M in annual revenue over the next 3-4 years. They recently advanced a 100 MW / 400 MWh Redbird project, acquired 79.2 MW of late-stage ERCOT assets, and partnered on an additional 480 MWh portfolio. The niche here is grid storage. AI data centers are creating enormous electricity demand and storage is becoming increasingly important for grid stability, renewable integration, and power availability. High risk, but the valuation disconnect is interesting. $VECO This might be my favorite of the group. One of the biggest AI bottlenecks isn’t compute anymore—it’s moving data efficiently. Veeco recently announced more than $250M in equipment orders tied to Indium Phosphide laser production for 800G and 1.6T optical transceivers used in AI infrastructure. FY26 revenue guidance is $740M-$800M and management expects compound semiconductor revenue to grow roughly 50% this year. The niche is optical connectivity. AI clusters need photonics, silicon photonics, and advanced networking to connect thousands of accelerators together. That’s where Veeco’s equipment comes in. $FCEL The earnings numbers weren’t pretty. Q2 revenue came in at $35.6M and the company posted a loss, but what caught my attention was the pipeline. Management reported a 4 GW sales pipeline, up 267% sequentially, with roughly 90% tied to data center opportunities. They also launched a standardized 12.5 MW Energy Block and are expanding manufacturing capacity toward 500 MW annually. The niche is data center power generation. Grid interconnection delays are becoming a major AI bottleneck and companies are looking for reliable behind-the-meter power solutions. Risky, but definitely worth watching. $ADUR This one isn’t directly AI related, but it solves a different infrastructure bottleneck. Aduro recently reported a 47-hour continuous pilot run with 86% liquid hydrocarbon yields and about 85% of output in the C20-and-below range suitable for existing naphtha cracker infrastructure. Cash sits around $39M and the company is advancing its first commercial-scale facility in Europe. The niche is industrial efficiency and feedstock processing. If successful, the technology could unlock value from waste streams that traditional recycling struggles to handle while integrating into existing petrochemical infrastructure. Not every opportunity has to be AI. Sometimes the biggest winners solve overlooked bottlenecks in large industries. Title: “Can Light Move a Machine?” Timestamp: June 9, 2026 Tagged: #Photonics #Robotics #Vulliez #DirectedEnergy #NorthstarLumen #WitnessScience #CanonicalLedger
⸻
⚖️ Core Proposition:
Can directed energy, light, or photonic systems cause robotics to move?
The answer is not science fiction.
It's already happening.
⸻
🧠 Forensic Findings:
Modern robotics routinely use:
• Lasers for navigation • LiDAR for environmental mapping • Optical sensors for positioning • Infrared guidance systems • Photonic communication networks • Machine vision driven by reflected light
Without light, many robotic systems become blind.
⸻
🔁 The Northstar+Lumen h-AI™ Effect-to-Cause™ Model
Effect:
A robotic system moves.
Cause:
The machine receives information.
That information may arrive through: • Radio frequency • Fiber optics • Cameras • Laser guidance • Infrared signals • Structured light projections
The machine is not responding to "light" alone.
It is responding to information carried by light.
⸻
🛰️ Why This Matters
Photonics is becoming one of the most important fields in robotics, autonomy, defense systems, manufacturing, transportation, and sensing technologies.
As sensors improve, machines increasingly interpret the world through optical inputs.
The question is no longer:
"Can light move a machine?"
The question is:
"How much of the machine's behavior depends upon what the light tells it to see?"

Like in this robotic raccoon that was positioned near a whistleblower’s former home? 
⸻
🧭 Northstar Observation
The future battlefield, factory floor, smart city, and autonomous vehicle all share a common dependency:
Light becomes data. Data becomes decisions. Decisions become motion.

text:
Expect some short term selling in photonics names on the CPO/800V delay headline. The market will paint the whole sector with one brush. 

Photonics is not just CPO.  CPO is one slice of a much larger stack.

Pluggable transceivers are a $26B market in 2026 growing to $45B by 2030. CPO was modeled at $0.1B this year. The delay barely touches the revenue that actually exists today.

LPO/LRO scales from $1.2B in 2026 to $5B by 2030 and just became more important.

> NPO is the one SemiAnalysis says could accelerate.  
>OCS grows from $1B to over $4B and saves power regardless of what happens with CPO.

Sell the headline if you want. But the TAM didn't move.
$AAOI $CRDO $SMTC $MTSI $COHR

text:
شركات النسخة الثانية من برنامج "صُمّم في السعودية بالذكاء الاصطناعي"

أعلنت كوالكوم، عبر شركتها التابعة Qualcomm Technologies International, Ltd.، وأرامكو السعودية، من خلال المختبر السعودي للابتكار المتسارع (aramcoSAIL)، وهيئة تنمية البحث والتطوير والابتكار @SaudiRDI ، وشركة هيوماين (HUMAIN)، عن الشركات الناشئة المختارة للمشاركة في النسخة الثانية من برنامج "صُمّم في السعودية بالذكاء الاصطناعي" (DISAI 2026).

ويهدف البرنامج إلى دعم الشركات الناشئة العاملة في مجال التقنيات العميقة والتي تطور حلولًا مبتكرة مدعومة بالذكاء الاصطناعي في المملكة.

ويقدم البرنامج الدعم للشركات الناشئة ذات الإمكانات العالية، بدءًا من مرحلة الفكرة وصولًا إلى التسويق التجاري.

الشركات الناشئة المختارة في النسخة الثانية من البرنامج: 

1- شركة Circula: شركة ناشئة سعودية 🇸🇦 توفر منصة اقتصاد دائري مدعومة بالذكاء الاصطناعي لأتمتة استرداد النفايات الصناعية وتعزيز الامتثال البيئي.

2- شركة دقة إيه أي (Deqa AI): شركة ناشئة سعودية 🇸🇦 تقدم منصة أتمتة مؤسسية تسهّل اتخاذ قرارات مدعومة بالذكاء الاصطناعي لتعزيز الكفاءة التشغيلية.

3- شركة ديجيتال بتروليوم (Digital Petroleum): شركة ناشئة سعودية 🇸🇦 طورت مستشعرًا صناعيًا مركزيًا مدعومًا بالذكاء الاصطناعي الطرفي يحل محل مستشعرات متعددة تقليدية ويُبسّط الرقمنة في إطار الجيل الرابع من الصناعة. 

4- شركة Electronic Photonics: شركة ناشئة سعودية 🇸🇦 توفر توأمًا رقميًا مروريًا مدعومًا بتقنية الليدار (LiDAR) والذكاء الاصطناعي الطرفي للتقاطعات الذكية وتحليلات التنقل.

5- شركة Finix Systems: شركة ناشئة سعودية 🇸🇦 تقدم وحدة تحكم طرفية معيارية لإنترنت الأشياء الصناعي لتحديث المعدات القديمة وتمكين المراقبة الذكية.

6- شركة نوماس إيه آي (Nommas ai): شركة ناشئة سعودية 🇸🇦 طورت "عقلًا مصنعيًا ذكيًا" يتيح فحص الجودة والصيانة الاستباقية وتعزيز السلامة على المستوى الطرفي.

7- شركة سدم (SDM): شركة ناشئة سعودية 🇸🇦 تقدم منصة مدعومة بالذكاء الاصطناعي لتشخيص الأمراض باستخدام التصوير الطبي للكشف المبكر.

8- شركة توكيد (Tawkeed): شركة ناشئة سعودية 🇸🇦 تقدم منصة ذكاء اصطناعي محلية لمعالجة الوثائق في الموقع، بالعربية أولًا، مع مراعاة متطلبات سيادة البيانات السعودية.

9- شركة Crosscall: شركة ناشئة فرنسية 🇫🇷 تقدم أجهزة تتبع قوية بتقنية الذكاء الاصطناعي للأشياء (AIoT) لشبكات 450 ميغاهرتز الصناعية وشبكات سلامة العمال.

10- شركة Dexabot: شركة ناشئة كندية 🇨🇦 متخصصة في الأمن الصناعي والمراقبة باستخدام الطائرات المسيّرة وأساطيلها.

الاصطناعي" أعلنت كوالكوم، عبر شركتها التابعة Qualcomm Technologies International, Ltd.، وأرامكو السعودية، من خلال المختبر السعودي للابتكار المتسارع (aramcoSAIL)، وهيئة تنمية البحث والتطوير والابتكار @SaudiRDI ، وشركة هيوماين (HUMAIN)، عن الشركات الناشئة المختارة للمشاركة في النسخة الثانية من برنامج "صُمّم في السعودية بالذكاء الاصطناعي" (DISAI 2026). ويهدف البرنامج إلى دعم الشركات الناشئة العاملة في مجال التقنيات العميقة والتي تطور حلولًا مبتكرة مدعومة بالذكاء الاصطناعي في المملكة. ويقدم البرنامج الدعم للشركات الناشئة ذات الإمكانات العالية، بدءًا من مرحلة الفكرة وصولًا إلى التسويق التجاري. الشركات الناشئة المختارة في النسخة الثانية من البرنامج: 1- شركة Circula: شركة ناشئة سعودية 🇸🇦 توفر منصة اقتصاد دائري مدعومة بالذكاء الاصطناعي لأتمتة استرداد النفايات الصناعية وتعزيز الامتثال البيئي. 2- شركة دقة إيه أي (Deqa AI): شركة ناشئة سعودية 🇸🇦 تقدم منصة أتمتة مؤسسية تسهّل اتخاذ قرارات مدعومة بالذكاء الاصطناعي لتعزيز الكفاءة التشغيلية. 3- شركة ديجيتال بتروليوم (Digital Petroleum): شركة ناشئة سعودية 🇸🇦 طورت مستشعرًا صناعيًا مركزيًا مدعومًا بالذكاء الاصطناعي الطرفي يحل محل مستشعرات متعددة تقليدية ويُبسّط الرقمنة في إطار الجيل الرابع من الصناعة. 4- شركة Electronic Photonics: شركة ناشئة سعودية 🇸🇦 توفر توأمًا رقميًا مروريًا مدعومًا بتقنية الليدار (LiDAR) والذكاء الاصطناعي الطرفي للتقاطعات الذكية وتحليلات التنقل. 5- شركة Finix Systems: شركة ناشئة سعودية 🇸🇦 تقدم وحدة تحكم طرفية معيارية لإنترنت الأشياء الصناعي لتحديث المعدات القديمة وتمكين المراقبة الذكية. 6- شركة نوماس إيه آي (Nommas ai): شركة ناشئة سعودية 🇸🇦 طورت "عقلًا مصنعيًا ذكيًا" يتيح فحص الجودة والصيانة الاستباقية وتعزيز السلامة على المستوى الطرفي. 7- شركة سدم (SDM): شركة ناشئة سعودية 🇸🇦 تقدم منصة مدعومة بالذكاء الاصطناعي لتشخيص الأمراض باستخدام التصوير الطبي للكشف المبكر. 8- شركة توكيد (Tawkeed): شركة ناشئة سعودية 🇸🇦 تقدم منصة ذكاء اصطناعي محلية لمعالجة الوثائق في الموقع، بالعربية أولًا، مع مراعاة متطلبات سيادة البيانات السعودية. 9- شركة Crosscall: شركة ناشئة فرنسية 🇫🇷 تقدم أجهزة تتبع قوية بتقنية الذكاء الاصطناعي للأشياء (AIoT) لشبكات 450 ميغاهرتز الصناعية وشبكات سلامة العمال. 10- شركة Dexabot: شركة ناشئة كندية 🇨🇦 متخصصة في الأمن الصناعي والمراقبة باستخدام الطائرات المسيّرة وأساطيلها. #الذكاء_الاصطناعي"}}}, text:
🚀Marvell Technology $MRVL stands out in amongst others in the AI infra:

Marvell is +300% past year as the market rewards their execution.

Not just another GPU play. Marvell dominates high-speed networking (800G/1.6T Ethernet, optical), custom ASICs/XPUs, and silicon photonics the critical “plumbing” powering massive AI data centers.

$NVDA invested $2B and brought them into NVLink Fusion. Jensen himself called them a potential next trillion-dollar company. Data center revenue exploding, custom silicon bookings strong, and FY2027 guidance raised again.

A name everyone should be watching closely.

text:
MicroVision $MVIS is quietly flying under the radar (LiDAR?). 

The reverse split and need for cash are holding the share price back for now, but signing a long-term development agreement with 🤫 Caterpillar 🤫 should put the LiDAR sector on notice.

All of the hype, technology, talent and most importantly, partnerships, that Luminar acquired over the years since their SPAC debut, is now all being rolled up under MicroVision Inc.

Caterpillar ✅
Volvo
Gatik
+PlusAI
Mercedes 

These are all Luminar deals/relationship that MicroVision CEO, Glen DeVos will look to repair heading into 2027. 

And they even future proofed the portfolio by acquiring 1550nm FMCW player, Scantinel Photonics. 

$MVIS is a ticker to watch! 👀

 🟢 $AMD +5.5% — Advanced Micro ($780B) ripping on the same BofA agentic note

🟢 $CPNG +13.2% — Coupang ($31B) still bouncing despite the $410M Korea fine
 🟢 $AAL +5.2% — American Airlines ($9B) extending into the afternoon

🔴 $ORCL -10.7% — Oracle ($517B) bleed continues but the tape moved past it; worst run in a quarter-century

🔝 Most active: $NVDA, $SMCI, $INTC. $SOXL +17.6% (3X semis ETF) telling you the magnitude.

text:
Hey thanks.  Sorry got pulled away.  Good stuff, this is the kind of pushback I was hoping for.

Fair on the 76M shares, may not move the needle especially if they don't take on more debt/dilution in the future.

The InP scarcity cutting both ways is a good point and I'm going to steal it. If InP is the bottleneck then qualified merchant capacity should give them pricing power. On this topic and the others, it seems like it will all come down to the photonics gross margin over the next couple of reports.  That may be the real tell. If substrate cost increases are passing through then they're a price maker instead of getting jammed up. The only wrinkle there is that the MACOM LTSAs were signed when IQE had no leverage, so I'd guess pass-through pricing power is capped on that slice of revenue.

Where I'd still push back a little is the "half of LandMark" comparison. LandMark at ~70x is arguably way overpriced, so half of a frenzy multiple is still frenzy math. And sales multiples can really only be compared between companies with similar margin structures, which is why I get stuck on the big margin gap.

Try it on EBITDA with your own numbers instead. 15% mature margin, give them 20% growth three years straight: that's ~£170M revenue and ~£25M EBITDA in FY28. At a generous 20x multiple that's ~£500M valuation. Market cap at 40p is ~£520M today. So the current price already embeds that mature-margin scenario two-plus years out, before discounting. Doesn't mean it can't work, but "undervalued" is tough until we some explosive margin growth/pricing power. The cheap entry was the 19.8p the insiders got.

Good thread either way. H1 interims plus photonics GM will settle a lot of this.

text:
INVESTORS CAN ABSORB MASSIVE SPACEX $SPCX, OPENAI & ANTHROPIC IPOS, SAYS SURO CAPITAL

SuRo Capital’s Willy Lee says investor demand is strong enough to absorb a $75B SpaceX IPO and $50B+ offerings from OpenAI and Anthropic, arguing public markets can handle AI listings of a scale already seen in private fundraising rounds.

Lee also pushed back on AI bubble concerns, noting investors continue to fund companies across the AI ecosystem — from memory chips and photonics to quantum computing and infrastructure — based on expectations of future demand rather than current revenue.

“We have a strong thesis around both AI and AI infrastructure,” Lee said.

text:
They do its call programable made of pure light 
============================================================
SOLIDIFICATION OF LIGHT AND PROGRAMMABLE MATTER
Connection Brief
============================================================

Solidification of Light:
- Refers to experiments making photons behave like condensed matter.
- Key breakthrough (March 2025): Italian researchers created the first
  "photonic supersolid" — light that has a rigid crystalline structure
  (solid-like) while flowing without friction (superfluid-like).
- Achieved using exciton-polaritons in semiconductor nanostructures.
Programmable Matter:
- Materials that can change physical properties (shape, density,
  optical traits, stiffness) in a controlled/programmable way.
- Includes metamaterials, shape-changing systems, and modular robotics.

Connections:
1. Light as a programmable medium: Supersolid light and polariton
   systems allow dynamic, reconfigurable quantum states controlled
   by lasers or external fields.

2. Programmable photonics overlap: Solid-light phenomena can serve
   as building blocks for adaptive optical chips, tunable lasers,
   optical neural networks, and quantum simulators.

3. Hybrid future tech: Combines exotic quantum behavior of "solid"
   light with the reconfigurability of smart materials, enabling
   potential applications in quantum computing, adaptive optics,
   and light-based programmable devices.

Current status: Mostly laboratory-scale quantum experiments, but
strong conceptual bridge toward future smart photonic materials.

References:
[1] Live Science / Nature (2025) - Photonic supersolid creation
[2] Wikipedia / ScienceAlert - Solid light and supersolids
[3] Wikipedia - Programmable matter
[4] Related works on polaritons and programmable photonics

text:
NVIDIA qui sort une démo de son Photonics co-packaged optics switch avec Lambda

À l’échelle GB300 NVL72, le réseau c’est littéralement ce qui détermine à quelle vitesse ton cluster « pense ».

Les co-packaged optics, c’est intégrer la photonique directement dans le switch. Moins de puissance consommée, moins de points de défaillance, et plus de tokens par watt.

Le genre de choses qui passent un peu sous le radar mais qui devient critique dès qu’on scale vraiment gros. Parce que quand tu as des milliers de GPUs qui doivent discuter ensemble, chaque watt gaspillé et chaque latence en trop te coûte cher en performance réelle.

NVIDIA construit toute la couche infra autour (compute + réseau + optique) pour que le système entier scale sans exploser en conso et en complexité.

Imo, c’est pile le genre de move qui renforce encore leur avantage sur les gros déploiements AI

text:
CMOS manufacturing and silicon photonics manufacturing operate at very different physical scales — and that changes the process-control conversation.
In advanced CMOS, critical dielectric features can be on the order of ~1.5–2 nm. There sub-nm variation can impact device performance. Typically in the form of threshold-voltage shift, which may still be electrically compensated in some cases.
In silicon photonics, waveguide features are much larger, typically 100s of  nm. Relative to Si electronics, a few nanometers of process tolerance in SiPho can still translate to tighter relative control, often below 1%.
Furthermore, the variation can impact optical performance, and unlike electronics, there may be limited or no electrical compensation available.
That is the key manufacturing tradeoff:
100× larger features. 10× tighter relative control. Unless you opt for post-fabrication correction.
As silicon photonics continues to scale into high-volume applications, mainly for optical interconnects, AI infrastructure, and co-packaged optics, manufacturing control will be just as critical as device design.
The challenge is not simply making smaller features.
It is making optical features predictable, repeatable, and manufacturable at scale.
The image is adapted from an excellent public presentation by GlobalFoundries.

In advanced CMOS, critical dielectric features can be on the order of ~1.5–2 nm. There sub-nm variation can impact device performance. Typically in the form of threshold-voltage shift, which may still be electrically compensated in some cases. In silicon photonics, waveguide features are much larger, typically 100s of nm. Relative to Si electronics, a few nanometers of process tolerance in SiPho can still translate to tighter relative control, often below 1%. Furthermore, the variation can impact optical performance, and unlike electronics, there may be limited or no electrical compensation available. That is the key manufacturing tradeoff: 100× larger features. 10× tighter relative control. Unless you opt for post-fabrication correction. As silicon photonics continues to scale into high-volume applications, mainly for optical interconnects, AI infrastructure, and co-packaged optics, manufacturing control will be just as critical as device design. The challenge is not simply making smaller features. It is making optical features predictable, repeatable, and manufacturable at scale. Maintains a clearly optimistic view on the future of the company

- High-performance wireless communications

- Defense applications

Like important strategic alliances:

- Partnership with $GFS

- Ayar Labs entering the $NVDA ecosystem

- Inclusion in the MSCI Small Caps

With 77% growth in a quarter

With the global AI boom as a backdrop

And backed by external validations such as CHIPS Act funding

The $SIVE shareholder invites interested parties to do their own research

Reminding that strong convictions often feel lonely before becoming obvious to the market

I couldn't agree more with their opinion

Bullish as ever on $SIVE
